content:
# WHO IMCI – Classification & Treatment Summary (2024‑2026) ## 1. Pneumonia (cough or difficult breathing) | Age group (child) | Fast‑breathing cut‑off* | Chest indrawing | General danger signs / other criteria that shift the classification to **Severe pneumonia / Very severe disease** | |-------------------|--------------------------|----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | **Young infant < 2 months** (0‑59 days) | ≥ 60 breaths / min (calm child) | Any chest indrawing | – Not able to drink or breast‑feed
– Vomits everything
– Convulsions (current or history)
– Lethargic or unconscious
– Stridor when the child is calm
– Severe malnutrition (weight‑for‑height < ‑3 z‑score or MUAC < 115 mm) | | **Infant 2 months – 12 months** | ≥ 50 breaths / min | Any chest indrawing | Same general danger signs as above (inability to drink, vomiting, convulsions, lethargy/unconscious, stridor, severe malnutrition) | | **Child 12 months – 59 months** | ≥ 40 breaths / min | Any chest indrawing | Same general danger signs as above | \*Fast‑breathing thresholds are taken from the standard IMCI chart (≥ 50 bpm for 2–12 mo; ≥ 40 bpm for 12–59 mo) and the young‑infant chart (≥ 60 bpm) [1][2]. ### 1.1 Classification Levels & First‑line Management | Classification (according to IMCI) | Clinical definition (key signs) | Recommended first‑line treatment (out‑patient) | |------------------------------------|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------------| | **No pneumonia (cough/cold)** | No fast breathing, no chest indrawing, no danger signs | Home care advice only; no antibiotics [2] | | **Pneumonia (non‑severe)** | Fast breathing **or** chest indrawing **without** any general danger sign | Oral amoxicillin 40 mg/kg **twice daily** for 3 days in low‑HIV‑prevalence settings (or 5 days where HIV prevalence is high) [2][3]. Dispersible tablets are preferred. | | **Severe pneumonia / Very severe disease** | Any **general danger sign** (inability to drink, persistent vomiting, convulsions, lethargy/unconscious, stridor in a calm child) **or** severe malnutrition | First dose of an appropriate injectable (e.g., ampicillin + gentamicin) **and urgent referral** to a hospital. Pre‑referral first‑dose oral amoxicillin may be given if injectable is unavailable [2][3]. | | **Very severe disease (any cough/difficult breathing with danger sign)** | Same as severe pneumonia; classified as “very severe disease” in the IMCI colour‑code (pink) | Same urgent referral and first‑dose injectable regimen as above [2]. | **Key points** * The 2020‑2022 WHO technical update merged the former “severe pneumonia” (chest indrawing) with “pneumonia” – both are now managed with oral amoxicillin unless a danger sign is present [3]. * In high‑HIV‑prevalence settings the course remains 5 days; in low‑HIV settings it may be shortened to 3 days [3]. * For children with wheeze, a rapid‑acting bronchodilator trial is recommended before classifying as pneumonia [1]. --- ## 2. Diarrhoea – Dehydration Classification | Dehydration level | Required clinical signs (≥ 2 of the listed) | IMCI classification | Core management (IMCI “Plan”) | |-------------------|-----------------------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------| | **Severe dehydration** | • Lethargic or unconscious
• Sunken eyes
• Not able to drink or drinking poorly
• Skin pinch goes back **very slowly** (> 2 s) | Severe dehydration | **Plan C** – Immediate IV rehydration (Ringer’s lactate or normal saline 100 ml/kg over 3 h for infants < 12 mo, 2.5 h for children ≥ 12 mo) plus first‑dose zinc; if oral intake possible, give ORS 5 ml/kg / hour while IV is running; urgent referral if IV not available [4][5]. | | **Some dehydration** | • Restless or irritable
• Sunken eyes
• Drinks eagerly / thirsty
• Skin pinch goes back **slowly** (≤ 2 s but slower than normal) | Some dehydration | **Plan B** – Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) 5 ml/kg / hour (or 75 ml/kg over 4 h) until the child can drink normally; give zinc 20 mg / day (10 mg for < 6 mo) for 10–14 days; continue feeding [6][7]. | | **No dehydration** | Fewer than two of the signs above; child may be thirsty but no sunken eyes, normal skin‑turgor, and able to drink normally | No dehydration | **Plan A** – Give extra fluids (preferably continued breastfeeding or ORS if needed), zinc for 10–14 days, maintain usual feeding, and advise when to return [6][7]. | ### 2.1 Additional IMCI considerations * **Blood in stool** → classify as **dysentery**; treat with a short course of an appropriate oral antimicrobial (per national policy) **plus** the dehydration plan that applies (Plan A/B/C) [6]. * **Persistent diarrhoea** (≥ 14 days) → assess for dehydration as above; if dehydration is present, manage as “severe/persistent diarrhoea” and refer for further evaluation [6]. * **Zinc** is recommended for **all** diarrhoea episodes, 20 mg daily (10 mg for infants < 6 months) for 10–14 days, regardless of dehydration status [7][8]. --- ## 3. Quick‑reference bullet lists ### 3.1 Pneumonia – Treatment actions by classification - **No pneumonia** – Advise home care; no antibiotics. - **Pneumonia (fast breathing ± chest indrawing, no danger sign)** – Give oral amoxicillin 40 mg/kg bd for 3 days (low‑HIV settings) or 5 days (high‑HIV); provide counselling on feeding, fever control, and when to return. - **Severe pneumonia / Very severe disease** – Give first‑dose injectable (ampicillin + gentamicin or ampicillin + chloramphenicol where indicated); give first oral dose of amoxicillin if possible; urgent referral to hospital; treat fever, maintain warmth, give pre‑referral treatment for hypoglycaemia if needed. ### 3.2 Diarrhoea – Treatment actions by dehydration level - **No dehydration (Plan A)** – Offer continued feeding (breast‑milk or age‑appropriate foods), give ORS if the child wants extra fluids, give zinc 20 mg (10 mg < 6 mo) for 10–14 days, educate caregiver on danger signs. - **Some dehydration (Plan B)** – Start ORS 5 ml/kg / hour (or 75 ml/kg over 4 h) until the child drinks well; give zinc as above; continue feeding; reassess after 4 h; advise caregiver on signs that require immediate return. - **Severe dehydration (Plan C)** – If IV access is available, give Ringer’s lactate 100 ml/kg over 3 h (infants < 12 mo) or 2.5 h (children ≥ 12 mo); if IV not possible, give rapid oral rehydration (ORS 5 ml/kg / hour) for 4 h then reassess; give first dose of zinc once oral intake resumes; refer urgently if shock persists or IV unavailable. --- **References used** Fast‑breathing thresholds, pneumonia classification & treatment: [1], [2], [3], [3]. Dehydration signs & management: [6], [4], [5], [7], [8]. --- ### Sources - [1] https://knowledgehub.health.gov.za/system/files/elibdownloads/2023-05/2022%20IMCI%20chart%20booklet_final.pdf - [2] https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK361919/table/part2.ch8.sec3.table3/ - [3] https://www.childhealthtaskforce.org/sites/default/files/2019-06/Advocacy%20Package_Pneumonia%20Evidence%20Brief%28WHO%2C%202014%29.pdf - [4] https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/preview/mmwrhtml/00018677.htm - [5] https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/978-92-4-154837-3 - [6] https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK143745/ - [7] https://www.who.int/tools/elena/commentary/zinc-diarrhoea - [8] https://www.defeatdd.org/wp-content/uploads/archive/DefeatDD_OnePager_ORS+Zinc_final.pdf
